reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Right to equal access to quality education regardless of family SES or ethnicity; child advocacy principle that decisions affecting disadvantaged pupils must be grounded in evidence, not assumption.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils currently excluded from selective tracks; families without resources for test preparation or social navigation.
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational segregation by age 10 entrenches inequality for life. A functional equity reform must remove barriers to high-ability low-SES pupils; partial measures that leave the sorting mechanism intact serve only to legitimise exclusion as 'merit-based'.
reactions.argument: S1 names the right problem — SES-skewed intake — but relies on three unverified assumptions that the scenario itself flags as weak or contradicted. (1) A less-coachable assessment: no evidence such an instrument exists or can be built; without it, renaming the exam changes nothing. (2) An SES proxy at application: Hungary has no student-level SES register, and any proxy is gameable; the scenario admits this. (3) That outreach alone raises application rates from low-SES families: no pilot data on efficacy. The scenario's own equity-impact statement says 'benefit is contingent and at best modest' and 'risks relabelling rather than reducing SES sorting'. For a child in a segregated primary school with no test-prep access, a reform that requires three unproven technical fixes is a promise without substance. Worse: it may entrench the narrative that 'we tried reform' while the real sorting mechanism — preparation inequality — remains untouched. We cannot support a measure that risks legitimising the status quo as 'reformed'.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would shift to support if: (1) a pilot assessment instrument is validated to be genuinely less sensitive to coaching (not just relabelled), with published psychometric data; (2) an SES proxy is piloted and audited for gaming resistance in a comparable system; (3) outreach efficacy is measured in a pilot cohort with documented uptake by SES/ethnicity before national roll-out; (4) the reform is bundled with mandatory quota enforcement on church/foundation schools. If any of these three-year pilots fail, the reform must not proceed.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Right to equal access to quality education; principle that postponing formal selection reduces SES-sorting at application (documented in comparative research); child advocacy that policy design must account for demonstrated risks of offset.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils currently selected out by age 10; pupils in general schools who benefit from reduced peer-composition stratification; rural primary schools losing selective-track pupils.
reactions.public_good_frame: Formal selection at 10 is too early to be meritocratic; it is a mechanism that converts family resources into track placement before the child has developed. Capping selective seats and retaining more pupils in a common track to age 14 is directionally correct. But the measure fails if non-state maintainers simply absorb the displaced selective demand — then we have renamed segregation, not reduced it.
reactions.argument: S2 is the only scenario that directly addresses the core inequity — early selection — with a mechanically sound measure: fewer selective seats means more pupils in the general track. The evidence on this mechanical effect is moderate-to-strong. However, the scenario's own risk analysis identifies a near-certain offset: 'Non-state maintainers expand selective intake to capture displaced demand, defeating the cap' (evidence: strong). The scenario flags this as 'the dominant risk' (our reading of the input). For a child whose family cannot afford private-school fees or church affiliation, a cap that is simply offset to the non-state sector is a motion, not a solution. We can support S2 only if the legislative package includes enforceable intake limits on church and foundation maintainers; without that, this is a shell game. The scenario itself notes that parallel intake limits on non-state schools have 'low confidence' of surviving legal and political challenge — that is precisely where we must insist the measure lives or dies.
reactions.condition_to_change: We will support S2 if and only if: (1) the legislation includes mandatory intake caps or SES-conscious admission rules on all maintainer types (state, church, foundation), with legal-policy modelling completed before passage to establish defensibility; (2) annual intake monitoring by maintainer type and school is published to track offset; (3) non-compliance triggers enforcement (eg. funding conditions). If non-state maintainers are exempt or if offset is not monitored, S2 is a false reform. If the offset risk materialises (non-state selective seats grow as state caps shrink), the measure has failed and must be escalated to S3.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Right to equal access to quality education; principle that postponing formal selection to age 14-15 reduces SES-sorting (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006 — documented evidence that early tracking harms equity without raising mean achievement); child advocacy that a child's potential is not determined at age 10.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils currently excluded from selective tracks by age 10-12; pupils in general schools who will benefit from reduced stratification; rural primary networks threatened by cohort drain to urban selective tracks; families whose cultural and linguistic resources do not match test-prep norms.
reactions.public_good_frame: Selection at age 10 is incompatible with educational equity because it converts family circumstances into irreversible track assignment before the child has developed the autonomy to resist or the information to choose. A comprehensive structure to age 14 with quality teaching and early support is the only reform that removes selection as a barrier rather than managing it. Sorting will not disappear — but formal, age-10, SES-sensitive sorting will be replaced with later, more-informed, family-choice-mediated sorting, which is a different and less damaging mechanism.
reactions.argument: S3 is the only scenario that addresses the root cause: early selection itself. The evidence is strong: Hanushek & Woessmann (2006) documents that earlier tracking raises inequality without raising mean achievement, and postponing selection is the mechanism. Poland's 1999 reform and Finnish comparatives show that comprehensive structures with teacher investment can reduce the SES-achievement gradient. Yes, the scenario flags major risks: (1) sorting may relocate to school choice and non-state exit — this is the core political risk; (2) teacher supply is a binding constraint; (3) political durability is weak. But these are implementation risks, not design flaws. A 10-year comprehensive pilot in selected districts with outcome tracking is feasible and would generate the confidence data the scenario flags as missing. For a child in a rural primary school or a low-SES urban family, S3 is the only reform that treats them as a pupil with potential, not a sorting statistic. We support S3 on the grounds that it is the only measure aligned with the evidence on equity.
reactions.condition_to_change: We will maintain support for S3 if: (1) implementation includes a 3-5 year pilot in selected urban and rural districts with comparison-group evaluation and outcome tracking to age 18, before national rollout; (2) teacher retraining and early-support investment are front-loaded and budgeted as non-negotiable before pilot launch (not contingent on 'workforce stabilisation'); (3) non-state maintainers are included in the comprehensive structure (no exemptions for church/foundation upper grades) or, if voluntary participation, the pilot is evaluated with explicit measurement of sorting relocation; (4) political ownership is documented in cross-party or multi-stakeholder commitment before pilot, with reversal-risk mitigation (eg. constitutional embedding or multi-year spending commitments). If the teacher supply is not stabilised or if pilot evaluation shows large-scale relocation of sorting to private/non-state sectors without countermeasures, we would revisit. But we would not abandon S3 in favour of S4 (compensation) — instead, we would escalate to full structural implementation with stronger non-state intake controls.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Right to equal access to quality education; principle that compensation without removal of barriers entrenches the sorting mechanism; child advocacy that a child in a segregated school does not benefit from 'higher-quality segregation'.
reactions.interest: Disadvantaged and Roma pupils in general schools would be materially better-off with additional funding and curriculum investment; but only if that investment does not legitimise their exclusion from selective tracks.
reactions.public_good_frame: Additional funding for disadvantaged schools is necessary but not sufficient. Without changing the selection mechanism, it is damage control, not equity reform. It treats symptoms — low outcomes in segregated schools — while leaving the cause — age-10 sorting by SES — intact. Worse: it may entrench segregation as permanent by framing it as 'manageable through compensation', when in fact segregation itself is the harm.
reactions.argument: S4 is a trap. The scenario's own language reveals it: 'Narrows the SES gradient at best partially' and 'mitigates symptoms rather than the cause'. Adding funding to general schools is good policy and necessary — but it is not equity reform if it leaves the sorting mechanism in place. For a talented child in a segregated primary school, additional TEIP-style funding in their general-track upper grade is a consolation prize, not a pathway. The scenario flags three fatal risks: (1) 'Ring-fenced equity funding is the first cut in fiscal consolidation' — meaning this reform is not durable; (2) 'Legitimising early selection permanently: compensation may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine'; (3) Teacher shortage blocks staffing of the package in exactly the disadvantaged schools it targets. These are not marginal risks — they are the core vulnerabilities of a non-structural approach. Portugal raised PISA outcomes (moderately), but Portugal did not have Hungary's degree of early formal selection; the transfer is not credible. S4 is politically attractive because it avoids confronting middle-class mobility anxiety and church-school interests. That is precisely why we must oppose it: it will be adopted as 'the equity solution' while the sorting mechanism remains untouched, and a generation of disadvantaged and Roma pupils will be tracked into segregated general schools with better funding, not with access.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would not shift to support S4 under any condition as a standalone measure. We would only accept S4 as a *complement* to S2 or S3 — that is, as targeted investment *after* early selection has been postponed or capped. If S4 were bundled with: (1) mandatory capping of selective seats on all maintainers (S2-type controls) *and* (2) a commitment that all compensatory funding is protected from budget cuts by statute or constitutional provision (not just 'intended' to be sustained), then additional funding would be welcome as a necessary complement. But as a standalone non-structural alternative, S4 is a mechanism for legitimising and managing segregation, not for ending it. We oppose.
